bug_data.description:
User Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64; rv:144.0) Gecko/20100101 Firefox/144.0

Steps to reproduce:

I've got this strange intermittent problem with SSL certificates on my personal website when accessing via the internal network. Firefox is not connecting over HTTPS, reporting SSL_ERROR_INTERNAL_ERROR_ALERT. However, this problem is not consistent.

When I'm getting this problem with Firefox, Chromium works fine, as does CURL. I'm running Caddy as a reverse proxy on Debian with Let's Encrypt certificates. All sites are affected when the issue occurs.

When Firefox works, it's recognising the certificates properly (i.e. it's not accessing the external site via Cloudflare Tunnel by accident) so I don't think it's a DNS issue. (I spent several days trying to troubleshoot my DNS (Pi-Hole) before realising the problem seems to be with Firefox).

Yesterday, everything was working properly. Today, Firefox does not connect. Nothing has changed on the server side, not even a reload/restart of Caddy. 

I'm running v144.0 on Linux (Arch). Only extension is Bitwarden which is disabled in Private Browser windows. The problem also occurs in these windows. Side-by-side PC's can have different results at the same time for the same site!

Second PC just had different results in different tabs for separate sites on the same internal Caddy server (i.e. one working, one not), although upon Shift-Refresh (but not just Refresh) of the working site/tab, it now fails.

I believe that after a certain amount of time (hours), the error resolves, but then re-occurs.


Actual results:

SSL_ERROR_INTERNAL_ERROR_ALERT


Expected results:

Page should load with proper HTTPS connection.

Further testing in private browser windows:
1) Attempt basic site (respond "Hello World") failed
2) Attempt reverse proxy app failed
Both of these sites were already failing in a regular window.
3) Attempt reverse proxy second app WORKED (first attempt to access this app during this session)
4) Shift-Refresh second app FAILED
5) Attempt reverse proxy third app WORKED (again, first access of this app during today)
6) Using app fine, including regular and Shift-Refresh.
7) Other tabs/apps still not working in Firefox. Everything working in Chromium.
8) On second PC, basic site failed as did the first app. Tried accessing second app for the first time today and this immediately failed. Third app working fine!!

comments.raw_text:
What is the DNS configuration for Firefox?  In particular is DoH enabled?   If it is, does disabling DoH fix it?

Could you collect network logs for this problem in a new profile?  You can create/launch a new profile via about:profiles, and set up logging via about:logging using the Networking preset.   Logging to a text file might be preferable here.  If you log via the profiler, you should first set the profiler to collect Networking profiles (to ensure it captures SocketThread).
You can send the logs to necko@mozilla.com

Thanks!

Here the last error is
```js
> ChromeUtils.getXPCOMErrorName(0x805a2f9c)
"NS_ERROR_GENERATE_FAILURE(NS_ERROR_MODULE_SECURITY, SSL_ERROR_INTERNAL_ERROR_ALERT)"
```

If I'm not mistaken, you have your DNS entries provided by cloudflare, which enables ECH. When resolving over the internet, you'll hit CF's server, but when the domain resolves to your local IP, we attempt to use ECH with cloudflare-ech.com in the SNI, which I assume your server rejects, causing the TLS issue.
When you are using DoH, how is the DNS for the server changed? Is it via /etc/hosts?

Here the last error is
```js
> ChromeUtils.getXPCOMErrorName(0x805a2f9c)
"NS_ERROR_GENERATE_FAILURE(NS_ERROR_MODULE_SECURITY, SSL_ERROR_INTERNAL_ERROR_ALERT)"
```

If I'm not mistaken, you have your DNS entries provided by cloudflare, which enables ECH. When resolving over the internet, you'll hit CF's server, but when the domain resolves to your local IP, we attempt to use ECH with cloudflare-ech.com in the SNI, which I assume your server rejects, causing the TLS issue.
When you are using DoH, how is the DNS for the server changed? Is it via /etc/hosts?

comments.author: djackson@mozilla.com
comments.raw_text:
My understanding was that we already didn't do DNS lookups for entries in `/etc/hosts`? I think we definitely want to be respecting those entries and not looking for HTTPS records if there's a hosts entries. 

I'm not sure about the merits of disabling ECH based on a local IP. On one hand it seems prudent - it's pretty likely folks don't intend to be using ECH there in the general case - but on the other hand it might make debugging a fair bit harder / less predictable. I guess it make sense to enable it and have a pref for disabling it. 

Do we understand why Chromium is working in this instance but we aren't?

comments.raw_text:
(In reply to Dennis Jackson from comment #12)
> My understanding was that we already didn't do DNS lookups for entries in `/etc/hosts`? I think we definitely want to be respecting those entries and not looking for HTTPS records if there's a hosts entries. 

We don't do DoH lookups for things in /etc/hosts - we don't have any such rule for native HTTPS lookups.

> I'm not sure about the merits of disabling ECH based on a local IP. On one hand it seems prudent - it's pretty likely folks don't intend to be using ECH there in the general case - but on the other hand it might make debugging a fair bit harder / less predictable. I guess it make sense to enable it and have a pref for disabling it. 
> 
> Do we understand why Chromium is working in this instance but we aren't?

No, it's possible because they're not doing HTTPS lookups for the /etc/hosts record.

User Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64; rv:144.0) Gecko/20100101 Firefox/144.0

Steps to reproduce:

I've got this strange intermittent problem with SSL certificates on my personal website when accessing via the internal network. Firefox is not connecting over HTTPS, reporting SSL_ERROR_INTERNAL_ERROR_ALERT. However, this problem is not consistent.

When I'm getting this problem with Firefox, Chromium works fine, as does CURL. I'm running Caddy as a reverse proxy on Debian with Let's Encrypt certificates. All sites are affected when the issue occurs.

When Firefox works, it's recognising the certificates properly (i.e. it's not accessing the external site via Cloudflare Tunnel by accident) so I don't think it's a DNS issue. (I spent several days trying to troubleshoot my DNS (Pi-Hole) before realising the problem seems to be with Firefox).

Yesterday, everything was working properly. Today, Firefox does not connect. Nothing has changed on the server side, not even a reload/restart of Caddy. 

I'm running v144.0 on Linux (Arch). Only extension is Bitwarden which is disabled in Private Browser windows. The problem also occurs in these windows. Side-by-side PC's can have different results at the same time for the same site!

Second PC just had different results in different tabs for separate sites on the same internal Caddy server (i.e. one working, one not), although upon Shift-Refresh (but not just Refresh) of the working site/tab, it now fails.

I believe that after a certain amount of time (hours), the error resolves, but then re-occurs.


Actual results:

SSL_ERROR_INTERNAL_ERROR_ALERT


Expected results:

Page should load with proper HTTPS connection.

comments.raw_text:
My understanding was that we already didn't do DNS lookups for entries in `/etc/hosts`? I think we definitely want to be respecting those entries and not looking for HTTPS records if there's a hosts entries. 

I'm not sure about the merits of disabling ECH based on a local IP. On one hand it seems prudent - it's pretty likely folks don't intend to be using ECH there in the general case - but on the other hand it might make debugging a fair bit harder / less predictable. I guess it make sense to enable it and have a pref for disabling it. 

Do we understand why Chromium is working in this instance but we aren't?

We don't do DoH lookups for things in /etc/hosts - we don't have any such rule for native HTTPS lookups.

bug_analysis.root_cause: Firefox's implementation of Encrypted Client Hello (ECH) causes SSL handshake failures when accessing internal network sites with local IP addresses, triggering SSL_ERROR_INTERNAL_ERROR_ALERT.
symptoms: Firefox intermittently fails to connect over HTTPS with SSL_ERROR_INTERNAL_ERROR_ALERT
Chromium and CURL connect successfully to the same sites
Errors occur inconsistently across different tabs and PCs
Error sometimes resolves after hours, then re-occurs
Shift-Refresh can trigger failure on previously working tabs
reproduction_steps: Access an internal website served via Caddy reverse proxy with Let's Encrypt certificates using Firefox v144.0 on Linux
Observe intermittent SSL_ERROR_INTERNAL_ERROR_ALERT errors during HTTPS connection attempts
Verify Chromium and CURL connect without issues to the same sites
Attempt Shift-Refresh on working tabs to observe failure
Use private browsing windows to reproduce inconsistent behavior
bug_analysis.technical_details: The server sends an internal_error TLS alert during handshake, likely caused by Firefox attempting ECH with hosts resolving to local IPs. Disabling ECH (network.dns.echconfig.enabled) or not using ECH for local addresses may mitigate the issue. DNS over HTTPS settings do not affect the failure. Logs show TLS handshake failing during NPN and socket control phases.
bug_analysis.user_impact: Users experience intermittent inability to establish secure HTTPS connections in Firefox to internal network sites, while other browsers work fine. This disrupts access to all internal sites served by the Caddy proxy, causing confusion and inconsistent behavior across devices and tabs.

refined_analysis.root_cause: Firefox's Encrypted Client Hello (ECH) causes TLS handshake failures (SSL_ERROR_INTERNAL_ERROR_ALERT) when connecting to HTTPS hosts resolving to local/private IP addresses.
symptoms: Intermittent SSL_ERROR_INTERNAL_ERROR_ALERT handshake failures in Firefox
No errors in Chromium or CURL for the same hosts
Failures sporadic across tabs and devices
Shift-Refresh triggers failure on previously working tabs
affected_components: SSL socket control in security/manager/ssl/nsSSLSocketProvider.cpp and nsNSSSocketControl.cpp
ECH handling integrated across netwerk/protocol/http and netwerk/dns
reproduction_steps: Use Firefox v144.0 on Linux to access internal HTTPS sites behind a Caddy reverse proxy with Let's Encrypt certs
Confirm Chromium and CURL connect successfully to same sites
Reproduce failures consistently by Shift-Refresh on working tabs
Verify failures occur in private browsing windows
refined_analysis.technical_details: TLS handshake fails with internal_error alert when Firefox attempts ECH to hosts resolving to local/private IPs. Disabling 'network.dns.echconfig.enabled' or excluding local IPs from ECH avoids failures. DNS-over-HTTPS settings do not influence the issue. Logs show failures during Next Protocol Negotiation (NPN) and socket control phases.
refined_analysis.user_impact: Users intermittently cannot establish secure HTTPS connections to internal network sites in Firefox, disrupting access to sites behind Caddy reverse proxy.
proposed_fix_approach: In netwerk/dns/DNS.cpp, detect hostnames resolving to local/private IP ranges and mark them to bypass ECH.
In netwerk/protocol/http/TlsHandshaker.cpp, modify TLS handshake flow to disable ECH for these marked hosts.
In security/manager/ssl/nsSSLSocketProvider.cpp and nsNSSSocketControl.cpp, honor ECH bypass flags during socket and SSL control.
Add a new configurable preference (e.g., 'network.dns.ech.bypass_internal') to toggle ECH bypass for local IPs.
Validate by reproducing failures and confirming successful TLS handshakes after the patch.
refined_analysis.optimized_context: Firefox's Encrypted Client Hello (ECH) causes intermittent TLS handshake failures (SSL_ERROR_INTERNAL_ERROR_ALERT) when connecting to HTTPS sites resolving to local/private IPs. Key code areas are netwerk/protocol/http/TlsHandshaker.cpp (TLS handshake logic), security/manager/ssl/nsSSLSocketProvider.cpp and nsNSSSocketControl.cpp (SSL socket control), and netwerk/dns/DNS.cpp (DNS resolution). Fix requires detecting local/private IPs during DNS resolution to bypass ECH and modifying handshake and socket control accordingly to restore reliable internal HTTPS connections.
refined_analysis.refinement_critique: The analysis is detailed and mostly self-contained with clear reproduction steps and affected components, but it is verbose and lacks references to specific functions or code blocks within the key files, limiting actionable precision. The reproduction and technical details can be condensed to improve clarity. The proposed fix is conceptually solid but would benefit from a concise, stepwise implementation plan. The optimized_context is currently too long and does not explicitly call out exact functions or code areas for focused fixes.

filtered_context.concise_summary: Firefox's Encrypted Client Hello (ECH) causes intermittent TLS handshake failures (SSL_ERROR_INTERNAL_ERROR_ALERT) when connecting to HTTPS hosts resolving to local/private IPs. To fix, detect hosts resolving to local/private IPs in netwerk/dns/DNS.cpp and mark them to bypass ECH. Modify TLS handshake logic in netwerk/protocol/http/TlsHandshaker.cpp and SSL socket control in security/manager/ssl/nsSSLSocketProvider.cpp and nsNSSSocketControl.cpp to honor this bypass. Add a preference (e.g., 'network.dns.ech.bypass_internal') to toggle bypassing ECH for local IPs.
core_symptoms: Intermittent SSL_ERROR_INTERNAL_ERROR_ALERT TLS handshake failures in Firefox to HTTPS hosts resolving to local/private IPs
No failures in Chromium or CURL for same hosts
Failures reproducible by Shift-Refresh and occur in private browsing
technical_constraints: TLS handshake fails with internal_error alert during ECH attempts to local/private IPs
Disabling ECH or excluding local IPs from ECH prevents failures
DNS-over-HTTPS settings do not affect issue
Fix requires coordinated changes in DNS resolution, TLS handshake, and SSL socket control to bypass ECH for local/private IP hosts
